title: Inguinal Hernia
oneLiner: A protrusion of abdominal contents through a weak point in the inguinal canal; common in men; risk of obstruction/strangulation makes timely recognition essential.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
You’re on call and a 68-year-old man presents with a two-day history of a lump in his right groin that appeared while lifting a heavy box. He now reports dull, crampy abdominal pain and nausea. He has not opened his bowels for 24 hours. On examination, you see a tense, tender, non-reducible swelling just above the inguinal ligament that does not transilluminate. Overlying skin is red and warm.

**Pattern Clues & What They Tell You:**
- **Lifting heavy box →** sudden increase in intra-abdominal pressure (typical precipitant for indirect hernia)
- **Non-reducible, tense, tender →** likely incarcerated hernia (contents trapped); tenderness suggests potential strangulation
- **No bowel motion for 24h + nausea →** possible bowel obstruction (complication of incarceration)
- **Red, warm overlying skin →** signs of inflammation/ischaemia; high suspicion for strangulation (especially if the patient is systemically unwell)

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**
- **Obese female with vague groin discomfort:** Femoral hernia can mimic inguinal; may present as a small, non-painful lump below the inguinal ligament. Often missed until strangulation occurs.
- **Infant with intermittent groin swelling only when crying:** Indirect inguinal hernia due to patent processus vaginalis – easily reducible, but risk of incarceration if not repaired.
- **Elderly man with chronic cough and bilateral groin “bulges”:** Direct hernias (weakness in Hesselbach’s triangle) are common in COPD patients; often bilateral, reducible, and rarely strangulate – but still need careful clinical assessment.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
An inguinal hernia is the protrusion of abdominal contents (omentum, bowel, bladder) through the inguinal canal – a natural passage in the lower abdominal wall. It is the most common hernia type, accounting for >75% of all abdominal wall hernias. Lifetime risk in men is ~27%, in women ~3%.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**
- **Frequency:** You will see one almost every on-call shift in general surgery or ED.
- **Stakes:** Approximately 5–10% of inguinal hernias become incarcerated; of those, ~30–50% become strangulated (ischaemia within 6–12 hours). Strangulated bowel requires emergency surgery – delay converts a straightforward repair into a bowel resection with significantly higher morbidity.
- **Common pitfalls:** Missed femoral hernia in women (misdiagnosed as “inguinal” but has higher strangulation risk – internal ring is narrower); failure to examine with patient standing/coughing; not appreciating that a “reducible” hernia can still be acutely incarcerated.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story of Development:**
The inguinal canal is an oblique passage through the abdominal wall, formed during testicular descent (males) or round ligament passage (females). In indirect hernias, a **patent processus vaginalis** (embryonic remnant) provides a pre-formed sac. Increased intra-abdominal pressure (lifting, coughing, straining) forces abdominal contents through this sac into the canal. In **direct hernias**, the canal itself is intact; instead, a weakening of the **transversalis fascia** in Hesselbach’s triangle (medial to inferior epigastric vessels) allows a bulge to push forward. Direct hernias are more common in older men and are rarely constricted at the neck.

**Mechanism → Sign:**
- **Earliest sign: “A lump that comes and goes”** – contents slide in and out of the defect; patient often feels it on standing/stooping.
- **“Expansile cough impulse”** – increased intra-abdominal pressure forces more contents into the sac, making the lump enlarge and then settle when pressure falls. Lack of impulse suggests incarceration or a mass unrelated to the hernia.
- **“Reducible”** – contents can be gently pushed back through the defect. If not reducible → incarcerated. If also tender, red, and the patient is unwell → strangulated.
- **Pain: Dull ache initially** – stretching of peritoneum and fascia. As ischaemia develops → sharp, constant pain with guarding and rebound.

**Connecting to Pattern Clues:**
- The patient in the vignette had a **tense, tender, non-reducible lump** – this is incarceration. The red skin and tenderness over the lump indicate ischaemia of the trapped bowel wall (strangulation). Nausea and obstipation are signs of bowel obstruction, which occurs in ~30% of incarcerated hernias.
- **Why does the bowel obstruct?** The hernia neck acts like a narrow ring – first, the lumen is compressed (obstruction), then the venous drainage is obstructed (congestion and oedema), then arterial inflow is cut off (strangulation). This sequence is why prompt reduction or surgery is critical.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Congenital** | Patent processus vaginalis (indirect hernia in children/young adults) | Infant with groin swelling when crying; reduced when supine |
| **Acquired: Increased intra-abdominal pressure** | Chronic cough (COPD, asthma), constipation, prostatism with straining, heavy lifting, ascites | Middle-aged/elderly man with chronic bronchitis or lifting injury |
| **Acquired: Weakness of abdominal wall** | Age, smoking (impairs collagen synthesis), connective tissue disease (e.g., Ehlers-Danlos), previous surgery (e.g., appendicectomy – can weaken nearby fascia) | Multiple risk factors present; bilateral hernias |
| **Iatrogenic** | Post-operative wound dehiscence, drain site herniation | Recent abdominal surgery |
| **Idiopathic** | Most adult indirect hernias in men have no clear cause | Otherwise healthy man with no identifiable risk factor |

**Most common cause:** In adult men, **idiopathic indirect hernia** (due to patent processus vaginalis that remained open from fetal life – ~80% of men have a patent sac at autopsy; most never herniate). Direct hernias are almost always acquired from age/weakness.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria:**
Inguinal hernia is primarily **clinical**. The key finding: *a reducible or non-reducible lump above the inguinal ligament with an expansile cough impulse.* No scoring system is widely used – presence determines management.

**For each test:**

| Test | What it Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Physical exam** | Lump above inguinal canal, cough impulse, reducibility | Confirms diagnosis; differentiates indirect vs direct (direct: medial to inferior epigastric vessels, reduces when defect is compressed over deep ring) | Reducible, non-tender → elective repair. Non-reducible, tender → emergency |
| **Ultrasound** | Hernia sac, contents (bowel, omentum, fluid), sac neck diameter | **When diagnosis is uncertain** (e.g., obese patient, female groin pain) or to distinguish from lymph node/cyst | Shows peristalsis within sac (diagnostic of hernia). Sac neck <1 cm is indirect; >1 cm is direct. High sensitivity (~96%) |
| **CT scan** | Same as US but with full abdominal survey | **Emergency setting** (suspected strangulation with bowel obstruction) – to confirm ischaemia (thickened bowel wall, free fluid, abnormal enhancement) or rule out other pathology (colonic carcinoma causing obstruction?) | Shows transition point, bowel wall thickening, pneumatosis (infarcted bowel). Useful for surgical planning |
| **Lactate** | Serum lactate >2 mmol/L | **If strangulation suspected** – ischaemic bowel produces lactate | Rising lactate >3–4 mmol/L suggests irreversible ischaemia and need for immediate laparotomy, not just hernia repair |
| **Plain abdominal X-ray** | Dilated bowel loops, air-fluid levels | **If obstruction is suspected** – but CT is superior | Can confirm small bowel obstruction (which may be due to incarcerated hernia) |

**Pattern-recognition finding that confirms / rules out:**
- **CONFIRMS hernia:** A reducible lump with an expansile cough impulse — no test needed.
- **RULES OUT hernia:** If on re-examination (standing, coughing) no lump is ever felt, and US shows no defect, then think of **obturator hernia** (rare, in females) or **femoral hernia** (below inguinal ligament).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – "Is bowel at risk?"**
- **Assess reducibility:** If tender, tense, and non-reducible → do not force reduction (risk of reducing ischaemic bowel).
- **Analgesia:** Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV if needed; but avoid heavy sedation until strangulation ruled out (may mask peritonitis).
- **IV fluids:** Hartmann’s 500 mL bolus if bowel obstruction or dehydration.
- **NBM** – nil by mouth in case surgery needed.
- **Urgent surgical referral** – if incarcerated/strangulated → aim for theatre within 2–4 hours.
- **Attempt gentle reduction (taxia)** – only if no tenderness, no peritonitis, and the patient is compliant. Place patient in Trendelenburg (15–20° head down), apply steady pressure over the hernia base for 10 minutes. If successful → observe 24h; failure → theatre.

**First-hour key: Do NOT attempt taxis if there is any red skin, systemic signs, or tenderness – you risk bowel perforation.**  

**Ward (24–72 hours) – For reducible hernias awaiting elective repair, or for emergency repairs for acutely incarcerated hernias**
- **Elective cases:** Schedule repair when symptomatic. Mesh repair (Lichtenstein tension-free) is gold standard – recurrence <1%.
- **Emergency cases (strangulated):** Laparotomy + resection of ischaemic bowel if non-viable (intraoperative assessment – look for colour, peristalsis, bleeding edges). Hernia repair with mesh is CONTRAINDICATED if bowel is resected (contaminated field → mesh infection). Use primary suture repair (Shouldice or Bassini) or Mayo repair.
- **Antibiotics:** If strangulated – give **co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV** (or cefuroxime + metronidazole if penicillin allergy) to cover skin and bowel flora. Duration: 24h if bowel viable, 5 days if resection.
- **Pain control:** Regular paracetamol 1 g PO QDS + ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (if no renal impairment). For open repair, local anaesthetic infiltration with bupivacaine 0.5% (max 2 mg/kg) for block.
- **Thromboprophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if non-ambulatory (risk of VTE after abdominal surgery).

**Long-term – What and Why**
- **Lifestyle advice:** Avoid heavy lifting for 6 weeks post-repair; treat constipation (lactulose 15 mL BD); manage chronic cough (bronchodilators, stop smoking – smoking slows collagen healing and increases hernia recurrence).
- **Watchful waiting:** For minimally symptomatic, reducible hernias in elderly men with multiple comorbidities – risk of strangulation is ~0.3% per year. Shared decision-making.
- **Mesh vs no mesh:** Mesh reduces recurrence (from ~10% with suture repair to <2%) but must not be placed in contaminated fields.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- **Reducing an incarcerated hernia blindly** – you can inadvertently reduce ischaemic bowel into the peritoneum, leading to delayed perforation and peritonitis.
- **Ordering CT for a straightforward hernia** – unnecessary radiation, cost, delay. CT only when diagnosis is uncertain or complications suspected.
- **Forgetting to examine the contralateral side** – bilateral hernias occur in ~20% of men; repair both during same admission if symptomatic.
- **Using tape/truss for treatment** – not recommended; trusses can cause atrophy, skin ulcers, and do not reduce strangulation risk.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Inguinal Hernia | Femoral Hernia | Lymph Node (Inguinal) | Hydrocele (of the cord) |
|---------|----------------|----------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Location** | Above inguinal ligament; may extend into scrotum (indirect) | Below inguinal ligament (medial to femoral pulse) | Anywhere along inguinal ligament; often multiple | In the scrotum; separate from testis |
| **Pain** | Vague ache; sharp if strangulated | Often painful; high strangulation risk (~40%) | Painless unless infected (then tender, red) | Dull, heavy feeling; no acute pain |
| **Exam** | Expansile cough impulse; reducible (non-complicated) | Non-reducible; small, firm, tender | Mobile, firm, matted; no cough impulse | Transilluminates (“fluid thrill”); non-reducible |
| **Cough impulse** | Present (if reducible) | Absent or minimal | Absent | Absent |
| **Risk of strangulation** | Low (indirect ~1–2% per year; direct even lower) | **High** (40% present with strangulation) | None | None |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | Above inguinal ligament; extends into scrotum (indirect) | Below inguinal ligament; never extends into scrotum | Firm, discrete mass; may have overlying skin changes (cellulitis) | Transilluminates; separate from testis; non-reducible |

**Additional differentials:** Saphena varix (varicosity of saphenous vein – larger, compressible, disappears on lying down); psoas abscess (groin swelling with fever, pain on hip extension); ectopic testis (absent testis in scrotum).
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major Complications:**
1. **Incarceration** – Hernia contents stuck, not reducible. Presents: tense, slightly tender lump, no systemic upset. Key: still have time to attempt gentle reduction (if no ischaemia). If untreated → progresses to strangulation.
2. **Strangulation** – Blood supply cut off. Presents: severe constant pain, redness over lump, tenderness, guarding, nausea/vomiting, fever, tachycardia. Key: lactate rises, WBC up, CT shows thickened bowel wall with poor enhancement. **What to do:** Urgent laparotomy – resect necrotic bowel, primary repair without mesh.
3. **Bowel Obstruction** – Can occur without strangulation. Presents: colicky abdominal pain, distension, high-pitched bowel sounds, obstipation. **Early sign:** the hernia becomes tense but still may be tender. **Treat:** NG tube, fluids, CT to rule out strangulation, then surgery.
4. **Recurrence** – After repair, rate ~1–5% with mesh, up to 10–20% with suture. Precipitants: smoking, obesity, chronic cough, early return to heavy lifting. Presents: lump small, reducible, often painless.

**Late-presentation patterns (“the exam stem that tests you”):**
- **Elderly man, known inguinal hernia for years, now with vomiting, obstipation, distended abdomen.** You find a tense, tender groin lump. This is a classic **small bowel obstruction** due to an incarcerated hernia – and it is a **surgical emergency**. Don’t be distracted by the distended abdomen; the hernia is the cause.
- **Woman with acute groin pain and a small, non-reducible lump below the ligament** – think femoral hernia. If missed, she returns with peritonitis from perforated ischaemic bowel.
- **Child with sudden-onset groin swelling and inconsolable crying + vomiting** – incarcerated indirect hernia. If not reduced within 4–6 hours, the testicular blood supply can be compromised (testicular torsion mimic – but the lump is above the testis, not in the scrotum itself).

**What to do if you suspect a late complication:** Immediate general surgical consult, NBM, IV fluids, CT abdomen and pelvis to assess ischaemia. Do not wait for a senior review without trending lactate and clinical exam every 30 minutes.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
1. **I N G U I N A L** (for indirect vs direct):
   - **I** ndirect – **I** nferior epigastric vessels **L** ateral (to the sac neck)
   - **D** irect – **D** irectly **M** edial to the inferior epigastric vessels (Hesselbach’s triangle)
2. **HERNIA** – for causes of increased intra-abdominal pressure: **H** eavy lifting, **E** xercise (straining), **R** espiratory (cough), **N** icturition (prostatism), **I** nfection (ascites), **A** scites / tumours.

**High-Yield Pearls:**
1. **“If you can get above the lump, it’s not a hernia.”** A true inguinal hernia originates in the inguinal canal – you cannot get above the upper limit of the swelling.
2. **Indirect hernias are more common in young men and children; direct hernias in older men (50+) with weak abdominal walls.**
3. **In women, any groin lump that is below the inguinal ligament is a femoral hernia until proven otherwise** – US is mandatory because femoral hernias have a much higher strangulation rate.
4. **A strangulated hernia will not have a bowel obstruction without a tender lump** – always examine the groin in any patient with small bowel obstruction.
5. **Do not reduce a hernia with a “gurgle” sign** – if you hear a gurgle, you’re reducing bowel that may be trapped – but if it was tender, you could be reducing ischaemic bowel. The gurgle is not reassuring.
6. **Post-operative urinary retention is common after inguinal hernia repair** – especially in men with prostatism. Monitor voiding, consider catheter if no urine output in 6 hours.
7. **Recurrence is rare after mesh repair; if it recurs, it’s usually at the medial edge of the mesh (direct recurrence) or through a missed femoral hernia.**

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Calling any groin lump “hernia” without checking the femoral region.
- Forgetting to examine the testicle – a retractile testis can mimic a hernia.
- Using a truss as definitive treatment – it provides no benefit and can cause skin damage/atrophy.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call a senior / emergency team:**
- **NEWS2 score ≥5** in a patient with known hernia – especially if the lump is new or has changed (tender, tense). This is a sepsis red flag.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** or rising rapidly – suggests bowel ischaemia → immediate general surgical consultant and consider ICU/anaesthetics for potential laparotomy.
- **GCS drop** (≤13) or hypotension (SBP <90) in setting of hernia – could be septic shock from peritonitis.
- **Any sign of peritonitis** (rigid abdomen, rebound, guarding) – this means bowel perforation has likely occurred; patient needs CRASH call and emergency theatre within 1 hour.
- **Failed reduction after one gentle attempt** (taxis) – do not repeat; senior must review for theatre.
- **Persistent vomiting / abdominal distension despite NG tube** – indicates full bowel obstruction; escalate to senior surgeon for expedited surgery.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS ≥5 –>** call FY2 / SpR immediately.
- **Lactate ≥4 –>** inform consultant surgeon and consider ICU bed (likely septic or ischaemic bowel).
- **GCS ≤13 + hernia →** treat as peritonitis – place on theatre list as emergency (within 2 hours).
- **Known hernia + sudden severe groin pain + inability to pass urine →** could be bladder involvement in an inguinal hernia (rare) – needs senior review and likely emergency repair.

**Bottom line for the junior doctor:** If you see a patient with a groin lump and any of the “red flags” (tenderness, redness, fever, tachycardia, vomiting, obstipation), do not send them home. Request a surgical review immediately. A strangulated hernia is a surgical emergency – your rapid recognition saves lives and bowel.

rawMarkdown:
## Inguinal Hernia
**One-liner:** A protrusion of abdominal contents through a weak point in the inguinal canal; common in men; risk of obstruction/strangulation makes timely recognition essential.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
You’re on call and a 68-year-old man presents with a two-day history of a lump in his right groin that appeared while lifting a heavy box. He now reports dull, crampy abdominal pain and nausea. He has not opened his bowels for 24 hours. On examination, you see a tense, tender, non-reducible swelling just above the inguinal ligament that does not transilluminate. Overlying skin is red and warm.

**Pattern Clues & What They Tell You:**
- **Lifting heavy box →** sudden increase in intra-abdominal pressure (typical precipitant for indirect hernia)
- **Non-reducible, tense, tender →** likely incarcerated hernia (contents trapped); tenderness suggests potential strangulation
- **No bowel motion for 24h + nausea →** possible bowel obstruction (complication of incarceration)
- **Red, warm overlying skin →** signs of inflammation/ischaemia; high suspicion for strangulation (especially if the patient is systemically unwell)

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**
- **Obese female with vague groin discomfort:** Femoral hernia can mimic inguinal; may present as a small, non-painful lump below the inguinal ligament. Often missed until strangulation occurs.
- **Infant with intermittent groin swelling only when crying:** Indirect inguinal hernia due to patent processus vaginalis – easily reducible, but risk of incarceration if not repaired.
- **Elderly man with chronic cough and bilateral groin “bulges”:** Direct hernias (weakness in Hesselbach’s triangle) are common in COPD patients; often bilateral, reducible, and rarely strangulate – but still need careful clinical assessment.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
An inguinal hernia is the protrusion of abdominal contents (omentum, bowel, bladder) through the inguinal canal – a natural passage in the lower abdominal wall. It is the most common hernia type, accounting for >75% of all abdominal wall hernias. Lifetime risk in men is ~27%, in women ~3%.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**
- **Frequency:** You will see one almost every on-call shift in general surgery or ED.
- **Stakes:** Approximately 5–10% of inguinal hernias become incarcerated; of those, ~30–50% become strangulated (ischaemia within 6–12 hours). Strangulated bowel requires emergency surgery – delay converts a straightforward repair into a bowel resection with significantly higher morbidity.
- **Common pitfalls:** Missed femoral hernia in women (misdiagnosed as “inguinal” but has higher strangulation risk – internal ring is narrower); failure to examine with patient standing/coughing; not appreciating that a “reducible” hernia can still be acutely incarcerated.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**The Story of Development:**
The inguinal canal is an oblique passage through the abdominal wall, formed during testicular descent (males) or round ligament passage (females). In indirect hernias, a **patent processus vaginalis** (embryonic remnant) provides a pre-formed sac. Increased intra-abdominal pressure (lifting, coughing, straining) forces abdominal contents through this sac into the canal. In **direct hernias**, the canal itself is intact; instead, a weakening of the **transversalis fascia** in Hesselbach’s triangle (medial to inferior epigastric vessels) allows a bulge to push forward. Direct hernias are more common in older men and are rarely constricted at the neck.

**Mechanism → Sign:**
- **Earliest sign: “A lump that comes and goes”** – contents slide in and out of the defect; patient often feels it on standing/stooping.
- **“Expansile cough impulse”** – increased intra-abdominal pressure forces more contents into the sac, making the lump enlarge and then settle when pressure falls. Lack of impulse suggests incarceration or a mass unrelated to the hernia.
- **“Reducible”** – contents can be gently pushed back through the defect. If not reducible → incarcerated. If also tender, red, and the patient is unwell → strangulated.
- **Pain: Dull ache initially** – stretching of peritoneum and fascia. As ischaemia develops → sharp, constant pain with guarding and rebound.

**Connecting to Pattern Clues:**
- The patient in the vignette had a **tense, tender, non-reducible lump** – this is incarceration. The red skin and tenderness over the lump indicate ischaemia of the trapped bowel wall (strangulation). Nausea and obstipation are signs of bowel obstruction, which occurs in ~30% of incarcerated hernias.
- **Why does the bowel obstruct?** The hernia neck acts like a narrow ring – first, the lumen is compressed (obstruction), then the venous drainage is obstructed (congestion and oedema), then arterial inflow is cut off (strangulation). This sequence is why prompt reduction or surgery is critical.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Congenital** | Patent processus vaginalis (indirect hernia in children/young adults) | Infant with groin swelling when crying; reduced when supine |
| **Acquired: Increased intra-abdominal pressure** | Chronic cough (COPD, asthma), constipation, prostatism with straining, heavy lifting, ascites | Middle-aged/elderly man with chronic bronchitis or lifting injury |
| **Acquired: Weakness of abdominal wall** | Age, smoking (impairs collagen synthesis), connective tissue disease (e.g., Ehlers-Danlos), previous surgery (e.g., appendicectomy – can weaken nearby fascia) | Multiple risk factors present; bilateral hernias |
| **Iatrogenic** | Post-operative wound dehiscence, drain site herniation | Recent abdominal surgery |
| **Idiopathic** | Most adult indirect hernias in men have no clear cause | Otherwise healthy man with no identifiable risk factor |

**Most common cause:** In adult men, **idiopathic indirect hernia** (due to patent processus vaginalis that remained open from fetal life – ~80% of men have a patent sac at autopsy; most never herniate). Direct hernias are almost always acquired from age/weakness.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic Criteria:**
Inguinal hernia is primarily **clinical**. The key finding: *a reducible or non-reducible lump above the inguinal ligament with an expansile cough impulse.* No scoring system is widely used – presence determines management.

**For each test:**

| Test | What it Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Physical exam** | Lump above inguinal canal, cough impulse, reducibility | Confirms diagnosis; differentiates indirect vs direct (direct: medial to inferior epigastric vessels, reduces when defect is compressed over deep ring) | Reducible, non-tender → elective repair. Non-reducible, tender → emergency |
| **Ultrasound** | Hernia sac, contents (bowel, omentum, fluid), sac neck diameter | **When diagnosis is uncertain** (e.g., obese patient, female groin pain) or to distinguish from lymph node/cyst | Shows peristalsis within sac (diagnostic of hernia). Sac neck <1 cm is indirect; >1 cm is direct. High sensitivity (~96%) |
| **CT scan** | Same as US but with full abdominal survey | **Emergency setting** (suspected strangulation with bowel obstruction) – to confirm ischaemia (thickened bowel wall, free fluid, abnormal enhancement) or rule out other pathology (colonic carcinoma causing obstruction?) | Shows transition point, bowel wall thickening, pneumatosis (infarcted bowel). Useful for surgical planning |
| **Lactate** | Serum lactate >2 mmol/L | **If strangulation suspected** – ischaemic bowel produces lactate | Rising lactate >3–4 mmol/L suggests irreversible ischaemia and need for immediate laparotomy, not just hernia repair |
| **Plain abdominal X-ray** | Dilated bowel loops, air-fluid levels | **If obstruction is suspected** – but CT is superior | Can confirm small bowel obstruction (which may be due to incarcerated hernia) |

**Pattern-recognition finding that confirms / rules out:**
- **CONFIRMS hernia:** A reducible lump with an expansile cough impulse — no test needed.
- **RULES OUT hernia:** If on re-examination (standing, coughing) no lump is ever felt, and US shows no defect, then think of **obturator hernia** (rare, in females) or **femoral hernia** (below inguinal ligament).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour) – "Is bowel at risk?"**
- **Assess reducibility:** If tender, tense, and non-reducible → do not force reduction (risk of reducing ischaemic bowel).
- **Analgesia:** Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV if needed; but avoid heavy sedation until strangulation ruled out (may mask peritonitis).
- **IV fluids:** Hartmann’s 500 mL bolus if bowel obstruction or dehydration.
- **NBM** – nil by mouth in case surgery needed.
- **Urgent surgical referral** – if incarcerated/strangulated → aim for theatre within 2–4 hours.
- **Attempt gentle reduction (taxia)** – only if no tenderness, no peritonitis, and the patient is compliant. Place patient in Trendelenburg (15–20° head down), apply steady pressure over the hernia base for 10 minutes. If successful → observe 24h; failure → theatre.

**First-hour key: Do NOT attempt taxis if there is any red skin, systemic signs, or tenderness – you risk bowel perforation.**  

**Ward (24–72 hours) – For reducible hernias awaiting elective repair, or for emergency repairs for acutely incarcerated hernias**
- **Elective cases:** Schedule repair when symptomatic. Mesh repair (Lichtenstein tension-free) is gold standard – recurrence <1%.
- **Emergency cases (strangulated):** Laparotomy + resection of ischaemic bowel if non-viable (intraoperative assessment – look for colour, peristalsis, bleeding edges). Hernia repair with mesh is CONTRAINDICATED if bowel is resected (contaminated field → mesh infection). Use primary suture repair (Shouldice or Bassini) or Mayo repair.
- **Antibiotics:** If strangulated – give **co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV** (or cefuroxime + metronidazole if penicillin allergy) to cover skin and bowel flora. Duration: 24h if bowel viable, 5 days if resection.
- **Pain control:** Regular paracetamol 1 g PO QDS + ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS (if no renal impairment). For open repair, local anaesthetic infiltration with bupivacaine 0.5% (max 2 mg/kg) for block.
- **Thromboprophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if non-ambulatory (risk of VTE after abdominal surgery).

**Long-term – What and Why**
- **Lifestyle advice:** Avoid heavy lifting for 6 weeks post-repair; treat constipation (lactulose 15 mL BD); manage chronic cough (bronchodilators, stop smoking – smoking slows collagen healing and increases hernia recurrence).
- **Watchful waiting:** For minimally symptomatic, reducible hernias in elderly men with multiple comorbidities – risk of strangulation is ~0.3% per year. Shared decision-making.
- **Mesh vs no mesh:** Mesh reduces recurrence (from ~10% with suture repair to <2%) but must not be placed in contaminated fields.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- **Reducing an incarcerated hernia blindly** – you can inadvertently reduce ischaemic bowel into the peritoneum, leading to delayed perforation and peritonitis.
- **Ordering CT for a straightforward hernia** – unnecessary radiation, cost, delay. CT only when diagnosis is uncertain or complications suspected.
- **Forgetting to examine the contralateral side** – bilateral hernias occur in ~20% of men; repair both during same admission if symptomatic.
- **Using tape/truss for treatment** – not recommended; trusses can cause atrophy, skin ulcers, and do not reduce strangulation risk.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | Inguinal Hernia | Femoral Hernia | Lymph Node (Inguinal) | Hydrocele (of the cord) |
|---------|----------------|----------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Location** | Above inguinal ligament; may extend into scrotum (indirect) | Below inguinal ligament (medial to femoral pulse) | Anywhere along inguinal ligament; often multiple | In the scrotum; separate from testis |
| **Pain** | Vague ache; sharp if strangulated | Often painful; high strangulation risk (~40%) | Painless unless infected (then tender, red) | Dull, heavy feeling; no acute pain |
| **Exam** | Expansile cough impulse; reducible (non-complicated) | Non-reducible; small, firm, tender | Mobile, firm, matted; no cough impulse | Transilluminates (“fluid thrill”); non-reducible |
| **Cough impulse** | Present (if reducible) | Absent or minimal | Absent | Absent |
| **Risk of strangulation** | Low (indirect ~1–2% per year; direct even lower) | **High** (40% present with strangulation) | None | None |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | Above inguinal ligament; extends into scrotum (indirect) | Below inguinal ligament; never extends into scrotum | Firm, discrete mass; may have overlying skin changes (cellulitis) | Transilluminates; separate from testis; non-reducible |

**Additional differentials:** Saphena varix (varicosity of saphenous vein – larger, compressible, disappears on lying down); psoas abscess (groin swelling with fever, pain on hip extension); ectopic testis (absent testis in scrotum).

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
**Major Complications:**
1. **Incarceration** – Hernia contents stuck, not reducible. Presents: tense, slightly tender lump, no systemic upset. Key: still have time to attempt gentle reduction (if no ischaemia). If untreated → progresses to strangulation.
2. **Strangulation** – Blood supply cut off. Presents: severe constant pain, redness over lump, tenderness, guarding, nausea/vomiting, fever, tachycardia. Key: lactate rises, WBC up, CT shows thickened bowel wall with poor enhancement. **What to do:** Urgent laparotomy – resect necrotic bowel, primary repair without mesh.
3. **Bowel Obstruction** – Can occur without strangulation. Presents: colicky abdominal pain, distension, high-pitched bowel sounds, obstipation. **Early sign:** the hernia becomes tense but still may be tender. **Treat:** NG tube, fluids, CT to rule out strangulation, then surgery.
4. **Recurrence** – After repair, rate ~1–5% with mesh, up to 10–20% with suture. Precipitants: smoking, obesity, chronic cough, early return to heavy lifting. Presents: lump small, reducible, often painless.

**Late-presentation patterns (“the exam stem that tests you”):**
- **Elderly man, known inguinal hernia for years, now with vomiting, obstipation, distended abdomen.** You find a tense, tender groin lump. This is a classic **small bowel obstruction** due to an incarcerated hernia – and it is a **surgical emergency**. Don’t be distracted by the distended abdomen; the hernia is the cause.
- **Woman with acute groin pain and a small, non-reducible lump below the ligament** – think femoral hernia. If missed, she returns with peritonitis from perforated ischaemic bowel.
- **Child with sudden-onset groin swelling and inconsolable crying + vomiting** – incarcerated indirect hernia. If not reduced within 4–6 hours, the testicular blood supply can be compromised (testicular torsion mimic – but the lump is above the testis, not in the scrotum itself).

**What to do if you suspect a late complication:** Immediate general surgical consult, NBM, IV fluids, CT abdomen and pelvis to assess ischaemia. Do not wait for a senior review without trending lactate and clinical exam every 30 minutes.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**
1. **I N G U I N A L** (for indirect vs direct):
   - **I** ndirect – **I** nferior epigastric vessels **L** ateral (to the sac neck)
   - **D** irect – **D** irectly **M** edial to the inferior epigastric vessels (Hesselbach’s triangle)
2. **HERNIA** – for causes of increased intra-abdominal pressure: **H** eavy lifting, **E** xercise (straining), **R** espiratory (cough), **N** icturition (prostatism), **I** nfection (ascites), **A** scites / tumours.

**High-Yield Pearls:**
1. **“If you can get above the lump, it’s not a hernia.”** A true inguinal hernia originates in the inguinal canal – you cannot get above the upper limit of the swelling.
2. **Indirect hernias are more common in young men and children; direct hernias in older men (50+) with weak abdominal walls.**
3. **In women, any groin lump that is below the inguinal ligament is a femoral hernia until proven otherwise** – US is mandatory because femoral hernias have a much higher strangulation rate.
4. **A strangulated hernia will not have a bowel obstruction without a tender lump** – always examine the groin in any patient with small bowel obstruction.
5. **Do not reduce a hernia with a “gurgle” sign** – if you hear a gurgle, you’re reducing bowel that may be trapped – but if it was tender, you could be reducing ischaemic bowel. The gurgle is not reassuring.
6. **Post-operative urinary retention is common after inguinal hernia repair** – especially in men with prostatism. Monitor voiding, consider catheter if no urine output in 6 hours.
7. **Recurrence is rare after mesh repair; if it recurs, it’s usually at the medial edge of the mesh (direct recurrence) or through a missed femoral hernia.**

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Calling any groin lump “hernia” without checking the femoral region.
- Forgetting to examine the testicle – a retractile testis can mimic a hernia.
- Using a truss as definitive treatment – it provides no benefit and can cause skin damage/atrophy.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call a senior / emergency team:**
- **NEWS2 score ≥5** in a patient with known hernia – especially if the lump is new or has changed (tender, tense). This is a sepsis red flag.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** or rising rapidly – suggests bowel ischaemia → immediate general surgical consultant and consider ICU/anaesthetics for potential laparotomy.
- **GCS drop** (≤13) or hypotension (SBP <90) in setting of hernia – could be septic shock from peritonitis.
- **Any sign of peritonitis** (rigid abdomen, rebound, guarding) – this means bowel perforation has likely occurred; patient needs CRASH call and emergency theatre within 1 hour.
- **Failed reduction after one gentle attempt** (taxis) – do not repeat; senior must review for theatre.
- **Persistent vomiting / abdominal distension despite NG tube** – indicates full bowel obstruction; escalate to senior surgeon for expedited surgery.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS ≥5 –>** call FY2 / SpR immediately.
- **Lactate ≥4 –>** inform consultant surgeon and consider ICU bed (likely septic or ischaemic bowel).
- **GCS ≤13 + hernia →** treat as peritonitis – place on theatre list as emergency (within 2 hours).
- **Known hernia + sudden severe groin pain + inability to pass urine →** could be bladder involvement in an inguinal hernia (rare) – needs senior review and likely emergency repair.

**Bottom line for the junior doctor:** If you see a patient with a groin lump and any of the “red flags” (tenderness, redness, fever, tachycardia, vomiting, obstipation), do not send them home. Request a surgical review immediately. A strangulated hernia is a surgical emergency – your rapid recognition saves lives and bowel.
